image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cashew/train_set/anthracnose3102/5978cashew_train_anthracnose.JPG

conversations.content: Look at this leaf — what disease is it?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Anthracnose**, caused by *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, twigs, and nuts. Leaves show irregular brown-black spots that may merge, causing defoliation. Flower blight reduces nut set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: multiple merging lesions covering 10-40% of leaf area, some twig dieback visible.
How can I save this crop?

For anthracnose in cashew: Prune and destroy infected branches. Apply copper-based fungicides (e.g., Bordeaux mixture) preventively before flowering. Use systemic fungicides like carbendazim or mancozeb during wet seasons. Ensure proper spacing for air circulation.
What preventive measures work against this?
To prevent anthracnose: Plant resistant varieties. Maintain canopy hygiene by removing fallen debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply fungicides at early flowering stage.

label: cashew_anthracnose

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/grasshoper/1830maize_train_grasshoper.JPG

conversations.content: Look at this leaf — what disease is it?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Grasshopper Damage**, caused by *Various grasshopper species (Orthoptera)*. Irregular, chewed leaf margins. Leaves may be stripped to the midrib in severe cases. Feeding starts from leaf edges inward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: severe defoliation across the field, many leaves reduced to midribs.
How can I save this crop?

For grasshopper damage in maize: Apply carbaryl or malathion for severe outbreaks. Use bait formulations (bran + insecticide) along field borders. Biological control with Nosema locustae (grasshopper pathogen). Hand-collect in small fields.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?

To prevent grasshopper damage: Maintain clean field borders to reduce egg-laying sites. Early planting reduces overlap with grasshopper hatching season. Encourage birds and other natural predators. Till soil after harvest to expose eggs.

label: maize_grasshoper

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive wilting, most lower leaves dead, stunting, reduced fruit production.
What's the best way to control this?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent verticillium wilt: Plant resistant varieties (look for 'V' designation). Practice long crop rotation (4+ years without solanaceous crops). Maintain soil pH around 6.5-7.0. Avoid overwatering and ensure good drainage.

What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent septoria leaf spot: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash. Avoid overhead watering. Stake plants for air circulation. Rotate crops — do not plant tomatoes in same spot for 3 years. Remove all plant debris at end of season.

Long, elliptical, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves. Northern type: cigar-shaped lesions 3-15 cm long. Southern type: smaller, tan, rectangular lesions. conversations.content: What disease does this plant have?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: slight yellowing on a few lower leaves, plant otherwise vigorous.
What treatment do you recommend?

How do I keep my crops safe from this?

To prevent verticillium wilt: Plant resistant varieties (look for 'V' designation). Practice long crop rotation (4+ years without solanaceous crops). Maintain soil pH around 6.5-7.0. Avoid overwatering and ensure good drainage.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf blight/801tomato_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: lesions on multiple leaf levels, some defoliation of lower canopy.
How can I save this crop?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

Gum exudation from stems. In severe cases, complete shoot dieback. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: widespread wilting, stem gum exudation, shoot dieback, plant collapse.
What treatment do you recommend?

For bacterial blight in cassava: Remove and burn infected plant parts immediately. There are no effective chemical treatments — management is primarily cultural. Use disease-free planting material. Rotate crops to break disease cycle.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent bacterial blight: Use certified disease-free stem cuttings. Plant resistant varieties (e.g., TME 419, IITA-improved lines). Avoid working in fields when plants are wet. Practice crop rotation with non-host crops for 2-3 seasons.

conversations.content: Can you identify what's wrong with this plant?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Gummosis**, caused by *Lasiodiplodia theobromae*. Exudation of gummy, resinous sap from trunk and branches. Bark cracking and peeling. Wilting and drying of affected branches. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: multiple branches showing gum exudation, some bark cracking.

For gummosis in cashew: Remove and burn heavily infected branches. Apply copper oxychloride paste to wounds after pruning. Improve tree vigor with balanced fertilization. Avoid mechanical injuries to the trunk.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?
To prevent gummosis: Avoid wounding trees during harvesting. Maintain tree health with proper nutrition. Apply Bordeaux paste to pruning wounds. Ensure good drainage around the root zone.

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/test_set/fall armyworm/97maize_valid_farmyw.JPG

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Fall Armyworm Damage**, caused by *Spodoptera frugiperda (insect pest)*. Ragged feeding damage on leaves with visible frass (excrement). Larvae bore into the whorl, causing 'windowpane' effect on leaves. Older larvae may bore into ears and tassels. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: multiple leaves with ragged holes, frass visible in whorl, small-medium larvae present.
How can I save this crop?

For fall armyworm damage in maize: Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays for early-stage larvae. Use emamectin benzoate or chlorantraniliprole for larger larvae. Apply into the whorl where larvae hide. Handpick larvae in small plots.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent fall armyworm damage: Plant early to avoid peak armyworm season. Use Bt-maize varieties where available. Scout fields weekly during vegetative stage. Encourage natural enemies (parasitic wasps, birds). Use pheromone traps for monitoring.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

Growing points may appear bushy due to shortened internodes. In dry conditions, leaves may appear scorched. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: noticeable leaf size reduction, moderate chlorosis on several leaves.
What treatment do you recommend?

For green mite damage in cassava: Introduce biological control agents (predatory mites like Typhlodromalus aripo). Apply sulfur-based or abamectin miticides for severe outbreaks. Irrigate plants during dry spells to reduce mite pressure.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent green mite damage: Use resistant/tolerant varieties. Plant early in the rainy season to establish before dry-season mite buildup. Avoid carrying infested planting material between fields. Conserve natural enemies.

label: cassava_green_mite

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/streak virus/683maize_train_streak virus.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Maize Streak Virus**, caused by *Maize Streak Virus (MSV), transmitted by leafhopper (Cicadulina mbila)*. Characteristic parallel streaks of pale yellow along leaf veins. Streaks are narrow and discontinuous. Stunted plant growth. Young plants are most severely affected. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: clear streaking on multiple leaves, some growth reduction.
What should I spray on this plant?
For maize streak virus in maize: No chemical cure. Remove and destroy severely infected plants. Control leafhopper vectors with pyrethroid insecticides. Focus management on early-stage protection.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent maize streak virus: Plant MSV-resistant varieties (most critical control). Adjust planting dates to avoid peak leafhopper activity. Control weeds that host leafhoppers. Use seed treatments with systemic insecticides.

label: maize_streak_virus

conversations.content: What disease does this plant have?

This cassava leaf shows signs of **Bacterial Blight**, caused by *Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. manihotis*. Angular, water-soaked leaf spots that turn brown. Wilting and drooping of shoot tips. Gum exudation from stems. In severe cases, complete shoot dieback. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: multiple leaves with expanding lesions, some tip wilting visible.
How can I save this crop?

For bacterial blight in cassava: Remove and burn infected plant parts immediately. There are no effective chemical treatments — management is primarily cultural. Use disease-free planting material. Rotate crops to break disease cycle.

label: cassava_bacterial_blight

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Anthracnose**, caused by *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, twigs, and nuts. Leaves show irregular brown-black spots that may merge, causing defoliation. Flower blight reduces nut set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few scattered dark spots on leaves, less than 10% leaf area affected.
What's the best way to control this?

For anthracnose in cashew: Prune and destroy infected branches. Apply copper-based fungicides (e.g., Bordeaux mixture) preventively before flowering. Use systemic fungicides like carbendazim or mancozeb during wet seasons. Ensure proper spacing for air circulation.

label: cashew_anthracnose

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/leaf beetle/1684maize_train_leaf beetle.JPG

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Beetle Damage**, caused by *Diabrotica spp. or Chaetocnema spp. (Chrysomelidae)*. Long, narrow feeding scars parallel to leaf veins. Small, elongated holes in leaves creating a 'scratch' pattern. Young plants are most susceptible. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive scarring, heavy feeding on young plants, potential silk clipping affecting pollination.
What treatment do you recommend?

For leaf beetle damage in maize: Apply contact insecticides (pyrethroids) if beetle counts exceed threshold. Seed treatments with neonicotinoids provide early-season protection. Target adult beetles during silk emergence to protect pollination.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent leaf beetle damage: Rotate crops (breaks rootworm beetle cycle). Use seed-treated varieties. Monitor beetle populations with yellow sticky traps. Maintain healthy, vigorous plants to tolerate moderate damage.

label: maize_leaf_beetle

Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: one-sided yellowing and wilting on several branches, reduced vigor.
How can I save this crop?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

How should I treat this?

For septoria leaf spot in tomato: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper. Begin applications at first sign of disease and repeat every 7-10 days. Remove and destroy infected lower leaves to slow spread.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent septoria leaf spot: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash. Avoid overhead watering. Stake plants for air circulation. Rotate crops — do not plant tomatoes in same spot for 3 years. Remove all plant debris at end of season.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf blight/2820tomato_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: rapid spread to upper canopy, fruit infection, potential total crop loss (especially late blight).
What treatment do you recommend?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.
What preventive measures work against this?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

conversations.content: What is affecting this plant's leaves?

What should I spray on this plant?
For leaf spot in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (mancozeb, chlorothalonil, or strobilurins) if disease is progressing rapidly. Usually economical only when lesions appear before silking.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?
To prevent leaf spot: Use resistant hybrids. Rotate crops. Manage residue through tillage. Avoid late planting which increases disease pressure.

label: maize_leaf_spot

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/test_set/fall armyworm/12maize_valid_farmyw.JPG

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Fall Armyworm Damage**, caused by *Spodoptera frugiperda (insect pest)*. Ragged feeding damage on leaves with visible frass (excrement). Larvae bore into the whorl, causing 'windowpane' effect on leaves. Older larvae may bore into ears and tassels. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few leaves showing small feeding holes, larvae just arrived.
What treatment do you recommend?

For fall armyworm damage in maize: Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays for early-stage larvae. Use emamectin benzoate or chlorantraniliprole for larger larvae. Apply into the whorl where larvae hide. Handpick larvae in small plots.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent fall armyworm damage: Plant early to avoid peak armyworm season. Use Bt-maize varieties where available. Scout fields weekly during vegetative stage. Encourage natural enemies (parasitic wasps, birds). Use pheromone traps for monitoring.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Maize Streak Virus**, caused by *Maize Streak Virus (MSV), transmitted by leafhopper (Cicadulina mbila)*. Characteristic parallel streaks of pale yellow along leaf veins. Streaks are narrow and discontinuous. Stunted plant growth. Young plants are most severely affected. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: severe stunting, extensive streaking on all leaves, poor ear development.
What should I spray on this plant?

What preventive measures work against this?

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Exserohilum turcicum (Northern Corn Leaf Blight) or Bipolaris maydis (Southern Corn Leaf Blight)*. Long, elliptical, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves. Northern type: cigar-shaped lesions 3-15 cm long. Southern type: smaller, tan, rectangular lesions. Lesions may coalesce, killing entire leaves. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: lesions on multiple leaf levels, some coalescing.
What treatment do you recommend?

What should I do to avoid this disease next season?
To prevent leaf blight: Plant resistant hybrids (Ht genes for NCLB resistance). Rotate with non-host crops. Till crop residue to reduce inoculum. Avoid continuous maize planting.

label: maize_leaf_blight

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: lesions on multiple leaf levels, some defoliation of lower canopy.
What treatment do you recommend?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/test_set/verticulium wilt/164tomato_valid_verticulium wilt.JPG

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: slight yellowing on a few lower leaves, plant otherwise vigorous.
What should I spray on this plant?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/leaf blight/2263maize_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: Is this a serious case?
This is a severe case. Immediate action is needed to prevent further spread and limit crop loss. Lesions on ear-leaf and above, extensive leaf death, yield loss expected.
What's the best way to control this?

For leaf blight in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, or pyraclostrobin) at first signs of disease above the ear leaf. Timing is critical — application before tasseling gives best ROI.

label: maize_leaf_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/leaf blight/3832maize_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Exserohilum turcicum (Northern Corn Leaf Blight) or Bipolaris maydis (Southern Corn Leaf Blight)*. Long, elliptical, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves. Northern type: cigar-shaped lesions 3-15 cm long. Southern type: smaller, tan, rectangular lesions. Lesions may coalesce, killing entire leaves. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: lesions on ear-leaf and above, extensive leaf death, yield loss expected.
How do I manage this disease?

For leaf blight in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, or pyraclostrobin) at first signs of disease above the ear leaf. Timing is critical — application before tasseling gives best ROI.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent leaf blight: Plant resistant hybrids (Ht genes for NCLB resistance). Rotate with non-host crops. Till crop residue to reduce inoculum. Avoid continuous maize planting.

label: maize_leaf_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/fall armyworm/983maize_train_farmyw.JPG

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Fall Armyworm Damage**, caused by *Spodoptera frugiperda (insect pest)*. Ragged feeding damage on leaves with visible frass (excrement). Larvae bore into the whorl, causing 'windowpane' effect on leaves. Older larvae may bore into ears and tassels. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, larvae in most whorls, potential ear damage.
What treatment do you recommend?

For fall armyworm damage in maize: Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays for early-stage larvae. Use emamectin benzoate or chlorantraniliprole for larger larvae. Apply into the whorl where larvae hide. Handpick larvae in small plots.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent fall armyworm damage: Plant early to avoid peak armyworm season. Use Bt-maize varieties where available. Scout fields weekly during vegetative stage. Encourage natural enemies (parasitic wasps, birds). Use pheromone traps for monitoring.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cashew/train_set/gumosis1714/1225cashew_train_gumosis.JPG

conversations.content: Should I be worried about this?
This is a severe case. Immediate action is needed to prevent further spread and limit crop loss. Extensive gum flow from the main trunk, branch dieback, canopy thinning.
How can I save this crop?

For gummosis in cashew: Remove and burn heavily infected branches. Apply copper oxychloride paste to wounds after pruning. Improve tree vigor with balanced fertilization. Avoid mechanical injuries to the trunk.

label: cashew_gumosis

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cashew/train_set/red rust4751/155cashew_train_red rust.JPG

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Red Rust**, caused by *Cephaleuros virescens (algal disease)*. Reddish-orange, velvety patches on upper leaf surface. Affected areas are slightly raised. Leaves may show premature aging around affected spots. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few small reddish patches on older leaves.
What's the best way to control this?
For red rust in cashew: Apply copper-based fungicides (copper hydroxide or copper oxychloride). Prune affected branches to improve air circulation. Apply during early onset for best results.

label: cashew_red_rust

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cassava/test_set/bacterial blight/1310cassava_valid_bb.JPG

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This cassava leaf shows signs of **Bacterial Blight**, caused by *Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. manihotis*. Angular, water-soaked leaf spots that turn brown. Wilting and drooping of shoot tips. Gum exudation from stems. In severe cases, complete shoot dieback. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: widespread wilting, stem gum exudation, shoot dieback, plant collapse.
How can I save this crop?

For bacterial blight in cassava: Remove and burn infected plant parts immediately. There are no effective chemical treatments — management is primarily cultural. Use disease-free planting material. Rotate crops to break disease cycle.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?

To prevent bacterial blight: Use certified disease-free stem cuttings. Plant resistant varieties (e.g., TME 419, IITA-improved lines). Avoid working in fields when plants are wet. Practice crop rotation with non-host crops for 2-3 seasons.

label: cassava_bacterial_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/grasshoper/2455maize_train_grasshoper.JPG

conversations.content: What disease does this plant have?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Grasshopper Damage**, caused by *Various grasshopper species (Orthoptera)*. Irregular, chewed leaf margins. Leaves may be stripped to the midrib in severe cases. Feeding starts from leaf edges inward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: minor leaf edge feeding on scattered plants.
What treatment do you recommend?

For grasshopper damage in maize: Apply carbaryl or malathion for severe outbreaks. Use bait formulations (bran + insecticide) along field borders. Biological control with Nosema locustae (grasshopper pathogen). Hand-collect in small fields.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent grasshopper damage: Maintain clean field borders to reduce egg-laying sites. Early planting reduces overlap with grasshopper hatching season. Encourage birds and other natural predators. Till soil after harvest to expose eggs.

label: maize_grasshoper

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Beetle Damage**, caused by *Diabrotica spp. or Chaetocnema spp. (Chrysomelidae)*. Long, narrow feeding scars parallel to leaf veins. Small, elongated holes in leaves creating a 'scratch' pattern. Young plants are most susceptible. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: multiple leaves showing feeding scars, some growth reduction.
What's the best way to control this?

For leaf beetle damage in maize: Apply contact insecticides (pyrethroids) if beetle counts exceed threshold. Seed treatments with neonicotinoids provide early-season protection. Target adult beetles during silk emergence to protect pollination.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent leaf beetle damage: Rotate crops (breaks rootworm beetle cycle). Use seed-treated varieties. Monitor beetle populations with yellow sticky traps. Maintain healthy, vigorous plants to tolerate moderate damage.

label: maize_leaf_beetle

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cashew/train_set/anthracnose3102/6295cashew_train_anthracnose.JPG

conversations.content: What disease does this plant have?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Anthracnose**, caused by *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, twigs, and nuts. Leaves show irregular brown-black spots that may merge, causing defoliation. Flower blight reduces nut set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few scattered dark spots on leaves, less than 10% leaf area affected.

label: cashew_anthracnose

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf curl/1367tomato_train_leaf curl.JPG

conversations.content: Look at this leaf — what disease is it?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Curl**, caused by *Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV), transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci)*. Upward curling and cupping of leaves. Leaves become leathery and crinkled. Yellow margins on leaves. Stunted plant growth. Flower drop and reduced fruit set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: severe stunting, most leaves curled and yellow, minimal or no fruit production.
What treatment do you recommend?

For leaf curl in tomato: No cure for infected plants. Remove and destroy severely infected plants. Control whiteflies with imidacloprid, pyriproxyfen, or neem oil. Use reflective mulch to repel whiteflies.

label: tomato_leaf_curl

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cashew/train_set/gumosis1714/412cashew_train_gumosis.JPG

conversations.content: How bad is this infection?
This is a moderate infection that needs attention soon. Treatment can still save the crop, but act quickly. Multiple branches showing gum exudation, some bark cracking.
How can I save this crop?

For gummosis in cashew: Remove and burn heavily infected branches. Apply copper oxychloride paste to wounds after pruning. Improve tree vigor with balanced fertilization. Avoid mechanical injuries to the trunk.

label: cashew_gumosis

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/fall armyworm/441maize_train_farmyw.JPG

conversations.content: Can you identify what's wrong with this plant?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Fall Armyworm Damage**, caused by *Spodoptera frugiperda (insect pest)*. Ragged feeding damage on leaves with visible frass (excrement). Larvae bore into the whorl, causing 'windowpane' effect on leaves. Older larvae may bore into ears and tassels. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, larvae in most whorls, potential ear damage.
What treatment do you recommend?

For fall armyworm damage in maize: Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays for early-stage larvae. Use emamectin benzoate or chlorantraniliprole for larger larvae. Apply into the whorl where larvae hide. Handpick larvae in small plots.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent fall armyworm damage: Plant early to avoid peak armyworm season. Use Bt-maize varieties where available. Scout fields weekly during vegetative stage. Encourage natural enemies (parasitic wasps, birds). Use pheromone traps for monitoring.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

conversations.content: Look at this leaf — what disease is it?

How should I treat this?

For grasshopper damage in maize: Apply carbaryl or malathion for severe outbreaks. Use bait formulations (bran + insecticide) along field borders. Biological control with Nosema locustae (grasshopper pathogen). Hand-collect in small fields.

conversations.content: What is affecting this plant's leaves?

How should I treat this?

How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive wilting, most lower leaves dead, stunting, reduced fruit production.
How can I save this crop?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent verticillium wilt: Plant resistant varieties (look for 'V' designation). Practice long crop rotation (4+ years without solanaceous crops). Maintain soil pH around 6.5-7.0. Avoid overwatering and ensure good drainage.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

conversations.content: Should I be worried about this?
This is a moderate infection that needs attention soon. Treatment can still save the crop, but act quickly. Lesions on multiple leaf levels, some coalescing.
What treatment do you recommend?

For leaf blight in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, or pyraclostrobin) at first signs of disease above the ear leaf. Timing is critical — application before tasseling gives best ROI.

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Exserohilum turcicum (Northern Corn Leaf Blight) or Bipolaris maydis (Southern Corn Leaf Blight)*. Long, elliptical, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves. Northern type: cigar-shaped lesions 3-15 cm long. Southern type: smaller, tan, rectangular lesions. Lesions may coalesce, killing entire leaves. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: lesions on multiple leaf levels, some coalescing.
What's the best way to control this?

For leaf blight in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, or pyraclostrobin) at first signs of disease above the ear leaf. Timing is critical — application before tasseling gives best ROI.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent leaf blight: Plant resistant hybrids (Ht genes for NCLB resistance). Rotate with non-host crops. Till crop residue to reduce inoculum. Avoid continuous maize planting.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cashew/test_set/anthracnose/1784cashew_valid_anthracnose.JPG

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Anthracnose**, caused by *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, twigs, and nuts. Leaves show irregular brown-black spots that may merge, causing defoliation. Flower blight reduces nut set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive necrosis, heavy defoliation, flower blight, and nut drop.
What should I spray on this plant?

For anthracnose in cashew: Prune and destroy infected branches. Apply copper-based fungicides (e.g., Bordeaux mixture) preventively before flowering. Use systemic fungicides like carbendazim or mancozeb during wet seasons. Ensure proper spacing for air circulation.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent anthracnose: Plant resistant varieties. Maintain canopy hygiene by removing fallen debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply fungicides at early flowering stage.

label: cashew_anthracnose

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

Small, elongated holes in leaves creating a 'scratch' pattern. Young plants are most susceptible. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: multiple leaves showing feeding scars, some growth reduction.
How do I manage this disease?

For leaf beetle damage in maize: Apply contact insecticides (pyrethroids) if beetle counts exceed threshold. Seed treatments with neonicotinoids provide early-season protection. Target adult beetles during silk emergence to protect pollination.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent leaf beetle damage: Rotate crops (breaks rootworm beetle cycle). Use seed-treated varieties. Monitor beetle populations with yellow sticky traps. Maintain healthy, vigorous plants to tolerate moderate damage.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/fall armyworm/69maize_train_farmyw.JPG

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This maize leaf shows signs of **Fall Armyworm Damage**, caused by *Spodoptera frugiperda (insect pest)*. Ragged feeding damage on leaves with visible frass (excrement). Larvae bore into the whorl, causing 'windowpane' effect on leaves. Older larvae may bore into ears and tassels. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, larvae in most whorls, potential ear damage.
What's the best way to control this?

For fall armyworm damage in maize: Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays for early-stage larvae. Use emamectin benzoate or chlorantraniliprole for larger larvae. Apply into the whorl where larvae hide. Handpick larvae in small plots.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

conversations.content: What disease does this plant have?

Larvae bore into the whorl, causing 'windowpane' effect on leaves. Older larvae may bore into ears and tassels. How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent fall armyworm damage: Plant early to avoid peak armyworm season. Use Bt-maize varieties where available. Scout fields weekly during vegetative stage. Encourage natural enemies (parasitic wasps, birds). Use pheromone traps for monitoring.

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few spots on lower/older leaves only.
How can I save this crop?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

Growing points may appear bushy due to shortened internodes. In dry conditions, leaves may appear scorched. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: severe leaf distortion, stunted growth, candle-stick appearance of growing points.
How do I manage this disease?

For green mite damage in cassava: Introduce biological control agents (predatory mites like Typhlodromalus aripo). Apply sulfur-based or abamectin miticides for severe outbreaks. Irrigate plants during dry spells to reduce mite pressure.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent green mite damage: Use resistant/tolerant varieties. Plant early in the rainy season to establish before dry-season mite buildup. Avoid carrying infested planting material between fields. Conserve natural enemies.

label: cassava_green_mite

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Exserohilum turcicum (Northern Corn Leaf Blight) or Bipolaris maydis (Southern Corn Leaf Blight)*. Long, elliptical, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves. Northern type: cigar-shaped lesions 3-15 cm long. Southern type: smaller, tan, rectangular lesions. Lesions may coalesce, killing entire leaves. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: lesions on multiple leaf levels, some coalescing.
How can I save this crop?

For leaf blight in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, or pyraclostrobin) at first signs of disease above the ear leaf. Timing is critical — application before tasseling gives best ROI.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?
To prevent leaf blight: Plant resistant hybrids (Ht genes for NCLB resistance). Rotate with non-host crops. Till crop residue to reduce inoculum. Avoid continuous maize planting.

label: maize_leaf_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/streak virus/3997maize_train_streak virus.JPG

conversations.content: Look at this leaf — what disease is it?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Maize Streak Virus**, caused by *Maize Streak Virus (MSV), transmitted by leafhopper (Cicadulina mbila)*. Characteristic parallel streaks of pale yellow along leaf veins. Streaks are narrow and discontinuous. Stunted plant growth. Young plants are most severely affected. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: severe stunting, extensive streaking on all leaves, poor ear development.
How do I manage this disease?
For maize streak virus in maize: No chemical cure. Remove and destroy severely infected plants. Control leafhopper vectors with pyrethroid insecticides. Focus management on early-stage protection.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent maize streak virus: Plant MSV-resistant varieties (most critical control). Adjust planting dates to avoid peak leafhopper activity. Control weeds that host leafhoppers. Use seed treatments with systemic insecticides.

label: maize_streak_virus

Leaves appear blistered with silvery or whitish patches. Heavily mined leaves curl and dry up. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few visible mines on scattered leaves, less than 10% of canopy affected.
How do I manage this disease?

For leaf miner in cashew: Spray neem oil or neem-based insecticides. Use systemic insecticides like monocrotophos for severe infestations. Remove and destroy heavily infested leaves. Encourage natural predators like parasitic wasps.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent leaf miner: Monitor for early signs of mining trails. Apply neem cake to soil around trees. Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes tender growth. Use pheromone traps for adult moths.

label: cashew_leaf_miner

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: one-sided yellowing and wilting on several branches, reduced vigor.
How do I manage this disease?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent verticillium wilt: Plant resistant varieties (look for 'V' designation). Practice long crop rotation (4+ years without solanaceous crops). Maintain soil pH around 6.5-7.0. Avoid overwatering and ensure good drainage.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few visible mines on scattered leaves, less than 10% of canopy affected.
What's the best way to control this?

For leaf miner in cashew: Spray neem oil or neem-based insecticides. Use systemic insecticides like monocrotophos for severe infestations. Remove and destroy heavily infested leaves. Encourage natural predators like parasitic wasps.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent leaf miner: Monitor for early signs of mining trails. Apply neem cake to soil around trees. Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes tender growth. Use pheromone traps for adult moths.

Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centers under magnification. Starts on lower leaves and moves upward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, spots reaching upper canopy, fruit exposed to sunscald.
How should I treat this?

How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent septoria leaf spot: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash. Avoid overhead watering. Stake plants for air circulation. Rotate crops — do not plant tomatoes in same spot for 3 years. Remove all plant debris at end of season.

How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent fall armyworm damage: Plant early to avoid peak armyworm season. Use Bt-maize varieties where available. Scout fields weekly during vegetative stage. Encourage natural enemies (parasitic wasps, birds). Use pheromone traps for monitoring.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf blight/5067tomato_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: rapid spread to upper canopy, fruit infection, potential total crop loss (especially late blight).
How should I treat this?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

conversations.content: I found this in my field. What's the problem?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Anthracnose**, caused by *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, twigs, and nuts. Leaves show irregular brown-black spots that may merge, causing defoliation. Flower blight reduces nut set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few scattered dark spots on leaves, less than 10% leaf area affected.
How should I treat this?

For anthracnose in cashew: Prune and destroy infected branches. Apply copper-based fungicides (e.g., Bordeaux mixture) preventively before flowering. Use systemic fungicides like carbendazim or mancozeb during wet seasons. Ensure proper spacing for air circulation.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent anthracnose: Plant resistant varieties. Maintain canopy hygiene by removing fallen debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply fungicides at early flowering stage.

label: cashew_anthracnose

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. How can I save this crop?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.

conversations.content: Look at this leaf — what disease is it?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Fall Armyworm Damage**, caused by *Spodoptera frugiperda (insect pest)*. Ragged feeding damage on leaves with visible frass (excrement). Larvae bore into the whorl, causing 'windowpane' effect on leaves. Older larvae may bore into ears and tassels. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few leaves showing small feeding holes, larvae just arrived.
What treatment do you recommend?

For fall armyworm damage in maize: Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays for early-stage larvae. Use emamectin benzoate or chlorantraniliprole for larger larvae. Apply into the whorl where larvae hide. Handpick larvae in small plots.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent fall armyworm damage: Plant early to avoid peak armyworm season. Use Bt-maize varieties where available. Scout fields weekly during vegetative stage. Encourage natural enemies (parasitic wasps, birds). Use pheromone traps for monitoring.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.

To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This maize leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Exserohilum turcicum (Northern Corn Leaf Blight) or Bipolaris maydis (Southern Corn Leaf Blight)*. Long, elliptical, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves. Northern type: cigar-shaped lesions 3-15 cm long. Southern type: smaller, tan, rectangular lesions. Lesions may coalesce, killing entire leaves. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: lesions on multiple leaf levels, some coalescing.
What's the best way to control this?

For leaf blight in maize: Apply foliar fungicides (azoxystrobin, propiconazole, or pyraclostrobin) at first signs of disease above the ear leaf. Timing is critical — application before tasseling gives best ROI.

label: maize_leaf_blight

Larvae bore into the whorl, causing 'windowpane' effect on leaves. Older larvae may bore into ears and tassels. conversations.content: What is affecting this plant's leaves?

Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centers under magnification. Starts on lower leaves and moves upward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, spots reaching upper canopy, fruit exposed to sunscald.
What's the best way to control this?

For septoria leaf spot in tomato: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper. Begin applications at first sign of disease and repeat every 7-10 days. Remove and destroy infected lower leaves to slow spread.
What preventive measures work against this?

Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. How can I save this crop?

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: slight yellowing on a few lower leaves, plant otherwise vigorous.
What should I spray on this plant?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?

To prevent verticillium wilt: Plant resistant varieties (look for 'V' designation). Practice long crop rotation (4+ years without solanaceous crops). Maintain soil pH around 6.5-7.0. Avoid overwatering and ensure good drainage.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cashew/test_set/anthracnose/718cashew_valid_anthracnose.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Anthracnose**, caused by *Colletotrichum gloeosporioides*. Dark, sunken lesions on leaves, twigs, and nuts. Leaves show irregular brown-black spots that may merge, causing defoliation. Flower blight reduces nut set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: a few scattered dark spots on leaves, less than 10% leaf area affected.
How should I treat this?

For anthracnose in cashew: Prune and destroy infected branches. Apply copper-based fungicides (e.g., Bordeaux mixture) preventively before flowering. Use systemic fungicides like carbendazim or mancozeb during wet seasons. Ensure proper spacing for air circulation.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?
To prevent anthracnose: Plant resistant varieties. Maintain canopy hygiene by removing fallen debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply fungicides at early flowering stage.

label: cashew_anthracnose

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cassava/train_set/mosaic/2493cassava_train_mosaic.JPG

This cassava leaf shows signs of **Mosaic Disease**, caused by *Cassava Mosaic Geminiviruses (CMGs), transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci)*. Distorted leaves with mosaic pattern of yellow and green patches. Leaf size reduction and curling. Stunted plant growth. Reduced tuber yield. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: clear mosaic on multiple leaves, noticeable size reduction and some curling.
What treatment do you recommend?

For mosaic disease in cassava: No chemical cure exists. Remove and destroy severely infected plants (roguing). Replant with virus-free, resistant cuttings. Control whitefly vectors with neem-based insecticides or yellow sticky traps.

label: cassava_mosaic

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/verticulium wilt/1640tomato_train_verticulium wilt.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: slight yellowing on a few lower leaves, plant otherwise vigorous.
How can I save this crop?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent verticillium wilt: Plant resistant varieties (look for 'V' designation). Practice long crop rotation (4+ years without solanaceous crops). Maintain soil pH around 6.5-7.0. Avoid overwatering and ensure good drainage.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This cassava leaf shows signs of **Mosaic Disease**, caused by *Cassava Mosaic Geminiviruses (CMGs), transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci)*. Distorted leaves with mosaic pattern of yellow and green patches. Leaf size reduction and curling. Stunted plant growth. Reduced tuber yield. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: light mosaic pattern on a few leaves, plant growth mostly normal.
How should I treat this?

For mosaic disease in cassava: No chemical cure exists. Remove and destroy severely infected plants (roguing). Replant with virus-free, resistant cuttings. Control whitefly vectors with neem-based insecticides or yellow sticky traps.

conversations.content: What disease does this plant have?

How can I save this crop?

For green mite damage in cassava: Introduce biological control agents (predatory mites like Typhlodromalus aripo). Apply sulfur-based or abamectin miticides for severe outbreaks. Irrigate plants during dry spells to reduce mite pressure.

For septoria leaf spot in tomato: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper. Begin applications at first sign of disease and repeat every 7-10 days. Remove and destroy infected lower leaves to slow spread.
How can I prevent this in the future?

To prevent septoria leaf spot: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash. Avoid overhead watering. Stake plants for air circulation. Rotate crops — do not plant tomatoes in same spot for 3 years. Remove all plant debris at end of season.

Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. What's the best way to control this?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.

Characteristic parallel streaks of pale yellow along leaf veins. Streaks are narrow and discontinuous. Stunted plant growth. Young plants are most severely affected. What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent maize streak virus: Plant MSV-resistant varieties (most critical control). Adjust planting dates to avoid peak leafhopper activity. Control weeds that host leafhoppers. Use seed treatments with systemic insecticides.

Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. What preventive measures work against this?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: slight yellowing on a few lower leaves, plant otherwise vigorous.
What's the best way to control this?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cashew/train_set/leaf miner3466/675cashew_train_leaf miner.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This cashew leaf shows signs of **Leaf Miner**, caused by *Acrocercops syngramma (insect pest)*. Serpentine mines (winding trails) visible on leaf surfaces. Leaves appear blistered with silvery or whitish patches. Heavily mined leaves curl and dry up. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: multiple mines per leaf on 10-30% of canopy, some leaf curling.
How can I save this crop?

For leaf miner in cashew: Spray neem oil or neem-based insecticides. Use systemic insecticides like monocrotophos for severe infestations. Remove and destroy heavily infested leaves. Encourage natural predators like parasitic wasps.

label: cashew_leaf_miner

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/septoria leaf spot/8863tomato_train_septoria leaf spot.JPG

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Septoria Leaf Spot**, caused by *Septoria lycopersici*. Numerous small (2-5 mm), circular spots with dark brown borders and gray centers. Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centers under magnification. Starts on lower leaves and moves upward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, spots reaching upper canopy, fruit exposed to sunscald.
How can I save this crop?

For septoria leaf spot in tomato: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper. Begin applications at first sign of disease and repeat every 7-10 days. Remove and destroy infected lower leaves to slow spread.

conversations.content: What disease does this plant have?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Septoria Leaf Spot**, caused by *Septoria lycopersici*. Numerous small (2-5 mm), circular spots with dark brown borders and gray centers. Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centers under magnification. Starts on lower leaves and moves upward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive defoliation, spots reaching upper canopy, fruit exposed to sunscald.
How should I treat this?

For septoria leaf spot in tomato: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper. Begin applications at first sign of disease and repeat every 7-10 days. Remove and destroy infected lower leaves to slow spread.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf blight/1846tomato_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: rapid spread to upper canopy, fruit infection, potential total crop loss (especially late blight).
How should I treat this?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Curl**, caused by *Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV), transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci)*. Upward curling and cupping of leaves. Leaves become leathery and crinkled. Yellow margins on leaves. Stunted plant growth. Flower drop and reduced fruit set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: severe stunting, most leaves curled and yellow, minimal or no fruit production.
What should I spray on this plant?

For leaf curl in tomato: No cure for infected plants. Remove and destroy severely infected plants. Control whiteflies with imidacloprid, pyriproxyfen, or neem oil. Use reflective mulch to repel whiteflies.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?

To prevent leaf curl: Use TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties. Install fine-mesh insect nets. Use yellow sticky traps. Transplant only healthy seedlings. Maintain a crop-free period to break the virus cycle.

label: tomato_leaf_curl

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf curl/1326tomato_train_leaf curl.JPG

conversations.content: Is this plant diseased? If so, what is it?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Curl**, caused by *Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV), transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci)*. Upward curling and cupping of leaves. Leaves become leathery and crinkled. Yellow margins on leaves. Stunted plant growth. Flower drop and reduced fruit set. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: severe stunting, most leaves curled and yellow, minimal or no fruit production.
What should I spray on this plant?

For leaf curl in tomato: No cure for infected plants. Remove and destroy severely infected plants. Control whiteflies with imidacloprid, pyriproxyfen, or neem oil. Use reflective mulch to repel whiteflies.
How do I keep my crops safe from this?

To prevent leaf curl: Use TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties. Install fine-mesh insect nets. Use yellow sticky traps. Transplant only healthy seedlings. Maintain a crop-free period to break the virus cycle.

label: tomato_leaf_curl

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/verticulium wilt/542tomato_train_verticulium wilt.JPG

conversations.content: What disease does this plant have?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Verticillium Wilt**, caused by *Verticillium dahliae*. Yellowing (chlorosis) starts on one side of the leaf or plant ('half-leaf' symptom). Lower leaves wilt and turn brown while upper leaves remain green initially. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: extensive wilting, most lower leaves dead, stunting, reduced fruit production.
What treatment do you recommend?

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent verticillium wilt: Plant resistant varieties (look for 'V' designation). Practice long crop rotation (4+ years without solanaceous crops). Maintain soil pH around 6.5-7.0. Avoid overwatering and ensure good drainage.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf blight/4623tomato_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: rapid spread to upper canopy, fruit infection, potential total crop loss (especially late blight).
How should I treat this?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

For verticillium wilt in tomato: No effective chemical treatment once plants are infected. Remove and destroy infected plants. Solarize soil in affected areas (cover with clear plastic for 4-6 weeks in hot weather). Add beneficial fungi (Trichoderma) to soil.

label: tomato_verticulium_wilt

Leaves may show premature aging around affected spots. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: multiple patches on 20-40% of leaves, some premature leaf drop.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/streak virus/595maize_train_streak virus.JPG

conversations.content: Diagnose this plant disease from the photo.

This maize leaf shows signs of **Maize Streak Virus**, caused by *Maize Streak Virus (MSV), transmitted by leafhopper (Cicadulina mbila)*. Characteristic parallel streaks of pale yellow along leaf veins. Streaks are narrow and discontinuous. Stunted plant growth. Young plants are most severely affected. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: severe stunting, extensive streaking on all leaves, poor ear development.
How should I treat this?
For maize streak virus in maize: No chemical cure. Remove and destroy severely infected plants. Control leafhopper vectors with pyrethroid insecticides. Focus management on early-stage protection.

label: maize_streak_virus

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Tomato/train_set/leaf blight/3063tomato_train_leaf blight.JPG

conversations.content: Can you identify what's wrong with this plant?

This tomato leaf shows signs of **Leaf Blight**, caused by *Alternaria solani (Early Blight) or Phytophthora infestans (Late Blight)*. Early blight: concentric ring ('target spot') lesions, starting on lower leaves. Late blight: large, water-soaked, dark brown lesions with white mold on undersides in humid conditions. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: rapid spread to upper canopy, fruit infection, potential total crop loss (especially late blight).
How should I treat this?

For leaf blight in tomato: Early blight: apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Late blight: apply metalaxyl/mancozeb immediately — late blight spreads extremely fast. Remove and destroy infected plant material. Do not compost infected tissue.
How can I prevent this in the future?
To prevent leaf blight: Stake and prune for air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Water in the morning at soil level. Rotate with non-solanaceous crops for 3+ years. Use resistant varieties.

label: tomato_leaf_blight

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/fall armyworm/266maize_train_farmyw.JPG

conversations.content: What's the severity level?
This looks like an early-stage infection. With prompt treatment, the plant should recover well. A few leaves showing small feeding holes, larvae just arrived.
How should I treat this?

For fall armyworm damage in maize: Apply Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) sprays for early-stage larvae. Use emamectin benzoate or chlorantraniliprole for larger larvae. Apply into the whorl where larvae hide. Handpick larvae in small plots.

label: maize_fall_armyworm

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Cassava/train_set/green mite/2836cassava_train_gmite.JPG

conversations.content: Can you identify what's wrong with this plant?

This cassava leaf shows signs of **Green Mite Damage**, caused by *Mononychellus tanajoa (spider mite)*. Pale yellow spotting (chlorosis) on young leaves. Leaves become reduced in size and distorted. Growing points may appear bushy due to shortened internodes. In dry conditions, leaves may appear scorched. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a severe case: severe leaf distortion, stunted growth, candle-stick appearance of growing points.
How do I manage this disease?

For green mite damage in cassava: Introduce biological control agents (predatory mites like Typhlodromalus aripo). Apply sulfur-based or abamectin miticides for severe outbreaks. Irrigate plants during dry spells to reduce mite pressure.
What preventive measures work against this?

To prevent green mite damage: Use resistant/tolerant varieties. Plant early in the rainy season to establish before dry-season mite buildup. Avoid carrying infested planting material between fields. Conserve natural enemies.

label: cassava_green_mite

Spots have a dark brown center with a lighter brown margin. Older leaves affected first. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a mild case: small scattered brown spots on lower leaves only.
How do I manage this disease?
For brown leaf spot in cassava: Apply copper-based fungicides or mancozeb. Remove and destroy severely infected lower leaves. Improve air circulation by adjusting plant spacing.

image: /workspace/data/ccmt/Dataset for Crop Pest and Disease Detection/CCMT Dataset-Augmented/Maize/train_set/grasshoper/1710maize_train_grasshoper.JPG

conversations.content: What condition is this plant suffering from?

This maize leaf shows signs of **Grasshopper Damage**, caused by *Various grasshopper species (Orthoptera)*. Irregular, chewed leaf margins. Leaves may be stripped to the midrib in severe cases. Feeding starts from leaf edges inward. Based on what I can see, this appears to be a moderate case: noticeable defoliation on many plants, some leaves stripped to midrib.
What's the best way to control this?

For grasshopper damage in maize: Apply carbaryl or malathion for severe outbreaks. Use bait formulations (bran + insecticide) along field borders. Biological control with Nosema locustae (grasshopper pathogen). Hand-collect in small fields.
What should I do to avoid this disease next season?

To prevent grasshopper damage: Maintain clean field borders to reduce egg-laying sites. Early planting reduces overlap with grasshopper hatching season. Encourage birds and other natural predators. Till soil after harvest to expose eggs.

label: maize_grasshoper
